response: The global food system faces mounting strain as a projected 9.7 billion people by 2050 will require a 60 percent increase in food production even as climate change drives down crop yields through droughts, floods, and extreme temperatures. Agriculture already accounts for roughly 25 percent of greenhouse gas emissions and suffers from soil degradation on one-third of arable land, while water scarcity—exemplified by the rapidly depleting Ogallala Aquifer—and food waste (about one-third of all produce) compound the crisis. Solutions under exploration include AI‐driven precision farming, urban vertical agriculture, alternative proteins, drought-resistant GM crops, and regenerative practices, alongside policy shifts in subsidies, trade, and carbon pricing. The central challenge is scaling these innovations and reforms in a way that keeps food both affordable and accessible to the world’s poorest.
